paragraphs.text: Since no law of virginity and chastity had yet been given, so that desire might not put any stain upon the soul of the righteous man, and since it had been said to Abraham that kings and nations would come from him, of which God also testified that He knew how Abraham would charge his sons and his sons’ sons to keep His commandments — after the death of Sarah Abraham took a concubine, with the aim that his many sons, scattering through many lands of all the earth, might by their piety spread the knowledge and honoring of the one God. And indeed Abraham had children also by Keturah; having given them gifts, he sent them away to the east, and died at the age of one hundred seventy-five years, and was buried with his wife Sarah.

And “God blessed Isaac” (Gen 25:11). And he “entreated the Lord for Rebekah… because she was barren.” And after twenty years “the Lord hearkened to him, and Rebekah conceived” (Gen 25:21). “And the children struggled within her… and she went to inquire of the Lord. And the Lord said unto her: Two nations are in thy womb” (Gen 25:22–23) — that is, the Edomite people and the Hebrew people. Whom she went to inquire of has been said by us above; for, speaking of Melchizedek, we have already said that it was him whom Rebekah went to inquire of. But she quickly returned home, because the pains of birth were already drawing near, and Rebekah bore Esau and Jacob.

And Jacob saw that Esau was despising his birthright, and began to plan how to make it his own, trusting in God, who had said: “the elder shall serve the younger” (Gen 25:23). And so one day Jacob was boiling lentils, and Esau comes from the hunt weary and says to Jacob: “Feed me, I pray thee, with that red red pottage” (Gen 25:30) — that is, let me taste somewhat of the lentils. “And Jacob said to him… Sell me… thy birthright” (Gen 25:31), and take everything for thyself. Esau swore and sold his birthright, and Jacob gave him the pottage, and he ate. And Scripture, showing that Esau sold his birthright not because of hunger, adds that, after he had eaten, “he rose up and went his way: and he despised his birthright” (Gen 25:34). So, not from hunger did he sell his birthright, but out of contempt; he sold it as something worthless, gave it away for nothing.
